prompt: How much tire degradation did we see in the last stint at Jerez?
expected_answer: Based on telemetry data from the last stint at Jerez, front-left tire degradation was 0.82s per lap with graining observed from lap 8. Rear tire temps stabilized after lap 3 at 98°C.

evidence_sources.title: Jerez FP2 Stint 4 Telemetry
evidence_sources.snippet: Front-left tire degradation: 0.82s per lap, graining onset at lap 8, rear tire temps: 98°C average.

prompt: What were the brake temperatures during FP2 at Barcelona?
expected_answer: Brake temperatures during FP2 at Barcelona averaged 650°C on the front left, peaking at 720°C in turn 9. Rear brakes were cooler at 480°C average.

evidence_sources.title: Barcelona FP2 Brake Sensor Log
evidence_sources.snippet: Front left brake: avg 650°C, peak 720°C (turn 9). Rear brakes: avg 480°C.

prompt: Analyze the understeer data from the high-speed corners at Silverstone
expected_answer: Understeer analysis at Silverstone shows a 3.2° steering angle deficit in turns 4, 9, and 15. Front camber is currently at -3.0°, suggesting a change to -3.5° with a corresponding 0.5-click stiffer front ARB.

evidence_sources.title: Silverstone Current Setup
evidence_sources.snippet: Front camber: -3.0°, front ARB: 4 clicks. Understeer index: 3.2° deficit in high-speed corners.

prompt: Adjust the rear suspension and camber for better traction at Monaco
expected_answer: For Monaco, I recommend increasing rear spring rate by 2 clicks and adjusting rear camber to -1.8° to improve traction over the kerbs. This change requires crew chief approval before applying.

evidence_sources.title: Monaco Base Setup
evidence_sources.snippet: Rear springs: 140 N/mm, rear camber: -2.2°, traction index: 4.2/10

prompt: What front wing profile do we need for the low-downforce Monza setup?
expected_answer: For Monza's low-downforce configuration, the LW-24 front wing profile with a 12° main plane and 3° flap angle is recommended. This matches the 2024 low-drag specification used successfully in Baku.

evidence_sources.title: Parts Catalog — Front Wings
evidence_sources.snippet: LW-24: low-drag profile, 12° main plane, 3° flap, suitable for Monza and Baku circuits.

prompt: Design new brake duct specifications for the Singapore GP
expected_answer: For Singapore GP, brake duct inlets should be 25% larger than standard (120mm vs 96mm) to manage the 22 braking events per lap. Recommended material: carbon-ceramic matrix with titanium heat shield.

evidence_sources.title: Singapore Brake Thermal Analysis
evidence_sources.snippet: 22 braking events per lap, peak energy 4.2 MJ/brake. Current duct: 96mm. Recommended: 120mm inlet.

prompt: Run a simulation to predict fuel consumption at Spa
expected_answer: Simulation results for Spa indicate a fuel consumption of 2.85 kg per lap at race pace. A full race distance of 44 laps would require 125.4 kg of fuel, with a 2% safety margin suggesting 128 kg.

evidence_sources.title: Spa Fuel Simulation Results
evidence_sources.snippet: Fuel consumption: 2.85 kg/lap race pace, 44 laps total, recommended fuel load: 128 kg.

prompt: Simulate the effect of increasing rear wing angle by 2 degrees on lap time at Silverstone
expected_answer: Increasing rear wing angle by 2° at Silverstone produces 0.12s gain in sector 2 (increased downforce through Maggotts-Becketts) but a 0.08s loss on the hangar straight. Net lap time impact: +0.04s.

evidence_sources.title: Silverstone Rear Wing CFD
evidence_sources.snippet: +2° rear wing: sector 2 gain 0.12s, hangar straight loss 0.08s, net +0.04s.

prompt: Generate a pre-GP crew chief report for the Interlagos race weekend
expected_answer: Interlagos pre-GP report: Weather forecast shows 60% rain probability for Sunday. Tire degradation is historically 0.9s per lap on the medium compound. Key setup concern is rear instability in turn 6.

evidence_sources.title: Interlagos Session History
evidence_sources.snippet: Rain probability: 60% Sunday. Tire deg: 0.9s/lap medium. Turn 6 rear instability flagged in 3 of last 5 races.

prompt: What are the current FIA regulations regarding minimum front wing weight?
expected_answer: FIA Technical Regulation Article 3.6 specifies a minimum front wing weight of 3.5 kg for the 2024 season. The weight includes all structural elements and must be verified at scrutineering.

evidence_sources.title: FIA 2024 Technical Regulations
evidence_sources.snippet: Article 3.6: Front wing minimum weight 3.5 kg including all structural elements.
